content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 6 – “P” for ProductPart 3 – Services marketing
Chapter 7 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Part 3 – Services marketing

Services marketing (1 of 7)
Nature and characteristics of a service
A company must consider four special service characteristics when designing marketing programmes:
service intangibility
service inseparability
service variability
service perishability.

Figure 7.4
Four service characteristics
Q: Due to service perishability, service firms often design strategies to enable a good match between demand and supply. Can you think of an example?

Services marketing (3 of 7)
Marketing strategies for service firms
Service firms establish their positions through traditional marketing mix activities.
However, as services differ from tangible products, they often also require additional marketing approaches beyond using the four Ps.
Successful service companies focus their attention on both their customers and their employees.
Q: What are the four Ps?
A: Product, Price, Promotion and Place.
The three additional Ps needed for Services are:
People, Processes and Premises.

Services marketing (4 of 7)
The service–profit chain
The service–profit chain links service firm profits with employee and customer satisfaction through:
internal service quality
satisfied and productive service employees
greater service value
satisfied and loyal customers
healthy service profits and growth.

Figure 7.5
Three types of service marketing

Services marketing (6 of 7)
The service–profit chain (cont.)
Internal marketing involves orienting and motivating customer-contact employees and supporting service people to work as a team to provide customer satisfaction.
Interactive marketing involves customers and others in the marketing channel on a one-to-one basis in person or remotely via digital technologies.
Q: Why is it important for service marketers to invest time and resources to ensure employee satisfaction? How is this different from product marketers?

Services marketing (7 of 7)
Managing service differentiation
As both price competition and costs increase, and as productivity and quality decrease, more service marketing sophistication is needed.
Service marketers have three main marketing tasks:
managing service differentiation: developing a differentiated offer, delivery and image
managing service quality: identifying and delivering what target customers expect in regard to service quality
managing service productivity.
Q: Are any of these marketing tasks more important than the others? If yes, which one(s)? If no, why not?

Services marketing
A: Travel industry – offer lower fares and room rates in the low season to encourage travel at times when most people would otherwise not travel.
Hospitality: Offer happy hours early in the evening to attract customers early.
Offer two dinner services per night. Early, say 6pm – 7:30pm at a lower price and 7:30pm – 9:30pm at a higher price.
Q: Due to service perishability, service firms often design strategies to enable a good match between demand and supply. Can you think of an example?

Services marketing
Q: Why is it important for service marketers to invest time and resources to ensure employee satisfaction? How is this different from product marketers?
A: In services marketing the employees deal directly with customers and deliver the service; retail sales assistants, hairdressers, public accountants etc. If the employees are satisfied, they will be more motivated to deliver their service in a more effective and positive manner.
Product marketers - most of the employees do not deal directly with the customers and do not deliver the product to the customers. (Exceptions would be sales representatives calling on retailers and wholesalers.)

Services marketing
Q: Service marketers have three main marketing tasks:
managing service differentiation: developing a differentiated offer, delivery and image
managing service quality: identifying and delivering what target customers expect in regard to service quality
managing service productivity.
Q: Are any of these marketing tasks more important than the others? If yes, which one(s)? If no, why not?
A: All tasks are important and work together to achieve sales and profits:
Service differentiation: the service offering must be made to be different to competitors in a way that will make our service more appealing than competitors’ services. Service quality is needed to ensure that the service delivered meets or exceeds the customers’ expectations, so that they will be satisfied. Service productivity is needed so that the business is profitable.

Coming up next
Part 4 - Branding strategy: Building strong brands
